infobox.value: ᠳᠤᠮᠳᠠᠳᠤ ᠤᠯᠤᠰ ᠤᠨ (ᠬᠢᠲᠠᠳ ᠤᠨ) ᠡᠪ ᠬᠠᠮᠲᠤ (ᠺᠣᠮᠮᠤᠶᠢᠨᠢᠰᠲ) ᠨᠠᠮ

(**CPC**),[[c]](#cite_note-4)[[2]](#cite_note-:62-5) commonly known as the **Chinese Communist Party** (**CCP**),[[3]](#cite_note-6) is the founding and [ruling party](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state") of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") (PRC). Founded in 1921, the CCP won the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") against the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") and [proclaimed the establishment of the PRC](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Proclamation of the People's Republic of China") under the [chairmanship](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Chairman of the Chinese Communist Party") of [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") in October 1949. The CCP has since governed China and has had sole control over the country's [armed forces](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") and [law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_China "Law enforcement in China"). As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Chinese_Communist_Party&action=edit), the CCP has more than 100 million members, making it the [second largest political party by membership in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_political_parties "List of largest political parties").

The **Communist Party of China** (**CPC**),[[c]](#cite_note-4)[[2]](#cite_note-:62-5) commonly known as the **Chinese Communist Party** (**CCP**),[[3]](#cite_note-6) is the founding and [ruling party](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state") of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") (PRC). Founded in 1921, the CCP won the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") against the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") and [proclaimed the establishment of the PRC](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Proclamation of the People's Republic of China") under the [chairmanship](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Chairman of the Chinese Communist Party") of [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") in October 1949. The CCP has since governed China and has had sole control over the country's [armed forces](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") and [law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_China "Law enforcement in China"). As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Chinese_Communist_Party&action=edit), the CCP has more than 100 million members, making it the [second largest political party by membership in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_political_parties "List of largest political parties").

In 1921, [Chen Duxiu](/wiki/Chen_Duxiu "Chen Duxiu") and [Li Dazhao](/wiki/Li_Dazhao "Li Dazhao") founded the CCP with the help of the Far Eastern Bureau of the [Russian Communist Party (Bolsheviks)](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union") and [Far Eastern Bureau of the Communist International](/wiki/Far_Eastern_Bureau_of_the_Communist_International "Far Eastern Bureau of the Communist International"). Although the CCP aligned with the Kuomintang (KMT) during its initial years, the rise of the KMT's right-wing under the leadership of [Chiang Kai-shek](/wiki/Chiang_Kai-shek "Chiang Kai-shek") and subsequent massacres of tens of thousands of CCP members resulted in a split and a prolonged civil war between the CCP and KMT. During the next ten years of [guerrilla warfare](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare"), Mao Zedong rose to become the most influential figure in the CCP and the party established a strong base among the rural [peasantry](/wiki/Peasant "Peasant") with its [land reform policies](/wiki/Land_Reform_Movement_(China) "Land Reform Movement (China)"). Support for the CCP continued to grow throughout the [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War"). After the [Japanese surrender](/wiki/Surrender_of_Japan "Surrender of Japan") in 1945, the CCP emerged triumphant in [the communist revolution](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Revolution "Chinese Communist Revolution") against the [Nationalist government](/wiki/Nationalist_government "Nationalist government"). The CCP established the People's Republic of China on 1 October 1949 and the remnants of the [Nationalist government retreated to Taiwan](/wiki/Retreat_of_the_government_of_the_Republic_of_China_to_Taiwan "Retreat of the government of the Republic of China to Taiwan") shortly after.

Mao Zedong continued to be the most influential member of the CCP until [his death in 1976](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Mao_Zedong "Death and state funeral of Mao Zedong"). Under Mao, the party completed its land reform program, launched a series of [five-year plans](/wiki/Five-year_plans_of_China "Five-year plans of China"), and eventually [split with the Soviet Union](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_split "Sino-Soviet split"). Although Mao attempted to purge the party of capitalist and [reactionary](/wiki/Reactionary "Reactionary") elements during the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution"), after his death, these policies were only briefly continued by the [Gang of Four](/wiki/Gang_of_Four "Gang of Four") before a less radical faction seized control. During the 1980s, [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping") directed the CCP away from Maoist orthodoxy and towards [reform and opening up](/wiki/Reform_and_opening_up "Reform and opening up"). Since the [collapse of the Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1989 "Revolutions of 1989") and the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union") in 1991, the CCP has focused on maintaining its relations with the ruling parties of the remaining [communist states](/wiki/List_of_communist_states "List of communist states"). The CCP has also established relations with several non-communist parties, including dominant nationalist parties of many developing countries in Africa, Asia and Latin America, as well as [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") parties in Europe.

A [Marxist–Leninist](/wiki/Marxist%E2%80%93Leninist "Marxist–Leninist") party, the Chinese Communist Party has termed its guiding political theories [socialism with Chinese characteristics](/wiki/Socialism_with_Chinese_characteristics "Socialism with Chinese characteristics") since 1982. The party is organized based on [democratic centralism](/wiki/Democratic_centralism "Democratic centralism"), a principle that entails open policy discussion on the condition of unity among party members in upholding the agreed-upon decision. The highest body of the CCP is the [National Congress](/wiki/National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party"), convened every fifth year. When the National Congress is not in session, the [Central Committee](/wiki/Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") is the highest body, but since that body usually only meets once a year, most duties and responsibilities are vested in the [Politburo](/wiki/Politburo_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Politburo of the Chinese Communist Party") and its [Standing Committee](/wiki/Politburo_Standing_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Politburo Standing Committee of the Chinese Communist Party"). Members of the latter are seen as the top leadership of the party and the state. Today the [party's leader](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Leader of the Chinese Communist Party") holds the offices of [general secretary](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party") (responsible for civilian party duties, also the [top rank official](/wiki/Order_of_precedence_in_China "Order of precedence in China")), [chairman of the Central Military Commission](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Central_Military_Commission_(China) "Chairman of the Central Military Commission (China)") (CMC) ([commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the military), and [president of China](/wiki/President_of_China "President of China") (a largely ceremonial position). Because of these posts, the party leader is seen as the country's "[paramount leader](/wiki/Paramount_leader "Paramount leader")". The current leader is [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping"), who was elected at the 1st Plenary Session of the [18th Central Committee](/wiki/18th_Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "18th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") held on 15 November 2012 and has been reelected twice, on 25 October 2017 by the [19th Central Committee](/wiki/19th_Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "19th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") and on 10 October 2022 by the [20th Central Committee](/wiki/20th_Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "20th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party").

## History

Main article: [History of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/History_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "History of the Chinese Communist Party")

### Founding and early history

The [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution") and [Marxist theory](/wiki/Marxist_philosophy "Marxist philosophy") inspired the founding of the CCP.[[4]](#cite_note-auto-7): 114 [Chen Duxiu](/wiki/Chen_Duxiu "Chen Duxiu") and [Li Dazhao](/wiki/Li_Dazhao "Li Dazhao") were among the first to publicly support [Leninism](/wiki/Leninism "Leninism") and [world revolution](/wiki/World_revolution "World revolution"). Both regarded the October Revolution in Russia as groundbreaking, believing it to herald a new era for oppressed countries everywhere.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_de_Ven199126–27-8)

Some historical analysis views the [May Fourth Movement](/wiki/May_Fourth_Movement "May Fourth Movement") as the beginning of the revolutionary struggle that led to the [founding of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Proclamation of the People's Republic of China").[[6]](#cite_note-9): 22 Following the movement, trends towards social transformation increased.[[7]](#cite_note-:04-10): 14 Writing in 1939, Mao Zedong stated that the Movement had shown that the [bourgeois](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") revolution against imperialism and China had developed to a new stage, but that the [proletariat](/wiki/Proletariat "Proletariat") would lead the revolution's completion.[[7]](#cite_note-:04-10): 20 The May Fourth Movement led to the establishment of radical intellectuals who went on to mobilize peasants and workers into the CCP and gain the organizational strength that would solidify the success of the [Chinese Communist Revolution](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Revolution "Chinese Communist Revolution").[[8]](#cite_note-zhidong-11) Chen and Li were among the most influential promoters of Marxism in China during the May Fourth period.[[7]](#cite_note-:04-10): 7 The CCP itself embraces the May Fourth Movement and views itself as part of the movement's legacy.[[9]](#cite_note-:9-12): 24

Study circles were, according to [Cai Hesen](/wiki/Cai_Hesen "Cai Hesen"), "the rudiments [of our party]".[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_de_Ven199138-13) Several study circles were established during the [New Culture Movement](/wiki/New_Culture_Movement "New Culture Movement"), but by 1920 many grew sceptical about their ability to bring about reforms.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_de_Ven199144-14) China's intellectual movements were fragmented in the early 1920s.[[12]](#cite_note-:12-15): 17 The May Fourth Movement and the New Culture Movement had identified issues of broad concern to Chinese progressives, including [anti-imperialism](/wiki/Anti-imperialism "Anti-imperialism"), support for [nationalism](/wiki/Chinese_nationalism "Chinese nationalism"), support for [democracy](/wiki/Democracy_in_China "Democracy in China"), promotion of [feminism](/wiki/Feminism_in_China "Feminism in China"), and rejection of traditional values.[[12]](#cite_note-:12-15): 17 Proposed solutions among Chinese progressives differed significantly, however.[[12]](#cite_note-:12-15): 17

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/The_First_National_Congress_of_CPC.jpg/250px-The_First_National_Congress_of_CPC.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_First_National_Congress_of_CPC.jpg)

[Site of the first CCP Congress](/wiki/Site_of_the_First_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Site of the First National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party"), in the former [Shanghai French Concession](/wiki/Shanghai_French_Concession "Shanghai French Concession")

The CCP's [founding congress](/wiki/1st_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "1st National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") commenced on 23 July 1921.[[13]](#cite_note-16)[[14]](#cite_note-:07-17): 229 At the time, there were 57 members of the CCP and 13 Chinese delegates present at the founding.[[15]](#cite_note-:14-18) While it was originally held in [a house](/wiki/Site_of_the_First_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Site of the First National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") in the [Shanghai French Concession](/wiki/Shanghai_French_Concession "Shanghai French Concession"), French police interrupted the meeting on 30 July[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGao2009119-19) and the congress was moved to a tourist boat on [South Lake](/wiki/South_Lake_(Jiaxing) "South Lake (Jiaxing)") in [Jiaxing](/wiki/Jiaxing "Jiaxing"), Zhejiang province.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGao2009119-19) Neither Li Dazhao nor Chen Duxiu attended,[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGao2009119-19) the latter sending a personal representative in his stead.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGao2009119-19) The resolutions of the congress called for the establishment of a [communist party](/wiki/Communist_party "Communist party") as a branch of the [Communist International](/wiki/Communist_International "Communist International") (Comintern) and elected Chen as its leader. Chen then served as the first general secretary of the CCP.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGao2009119-19)

The Soviets hoped to foster pro-Soviet forces in East Asia to fight against [anti-communist](/wiki/Anti-communist "Anti-communist") countries, particularly [Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan"). They attempted to contact the warlord [Wu Peifu](/wiki/Wu_Peifu "Wu Peifu") but failed.[[17]](#cite_note-20)[[18]](#cite_note-21) The Soviets then contacted the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") (KMT), which was leading the [Guangzhou government](/wiki/Government_of_the_Republic_of_China_in_Guangzhou "Government of the Republic of China in Guangzhou") parallel to the [Beiyang government](/wiki/Beiyang_government "Beiyang government"). On 6 October 1923, the Comintern sent [Mikhail Borodin](/wiki/Mikhail_Borodin "Mikhail Borodin") to Guangzhou, and the Soviets established friendly relations with the KMT. The [Central Committee of the CCP](/wiki/Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party"),[[19]](#cite_note-22) Soviet leader [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin"),[[20]](#cite_note-23) and the Comintern[[21]](#cite_note-24) all hoped that the CCP would eventually control the KMT and called their opponents "rightists".[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchram196684,_89-25)[[d]](#cite_note-27) KMT leader [Sun Yat-sen](/wiki/Sun_Yat-sen "Sun Yat-sen") eased the conflict between the communists and their opponents. CCP membership grew tremendously after the [4th congress](/wiki/4th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "4th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") in 1925, from 900 to 2,428.[[24]](#cite_note-ksy-28) The CCP still treats Sun Yat-sen as one of the founders of their movement and claim descent from him[[25]](#cite_note-29) as he is viewed as a proto-communist[[26]](#cite_note-30) and the economic element of [Sun's ideology](/wiki/Three_Principles_of_the_People "Three Principles of the People") was socialism.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDirlik2005[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidS-aGLEtx7AYCpgPA20_20]-31) Sun stated, "Our Principle of Livelihood is a form of communism".[[28]](#cite_note-32)

The communists dominated the [left wing of the KMT](/wiki/Socialist_ideology_of_the_Kuomintang "Socialist ideology of the Kuomintang") and struggled for power with the party's right-wing factions.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchram196684,_89-25) When Sun Yat-sen died in March 1925, he was succeeded by a rightist, [Chiang Kai-shek](/wiki/Chiang_Kai-shek "Chiang Kai-shek"), who initiated moves to marginalize the position of the communists.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchram196684,_89-25) Chiang, Sun's former assistant, was not actively anti-communist at that time,[[29]](#cite_note-33) even though he hated the theory of [class struggle](/wiki/Class_conflict "Class conflict") and the CCP's seizure of power.[[23]](#cite_note-ccc-26) The communists proposed removing Chiang's power.[[30]](#cite_note-34) When Chiang gradually gained the support of Western countries, the conflict between him and the communists became more and more intense. Chiang asked the Kuomintang to join the Comintern to rule out the secret expansion of communists within the KMT, while Chen Duxiu hoped that the communists would completely withdraw from the KMT.[[31]](#cite_note-35)

In April 1927, both Chiang and the CCP were preparing for conflict.[[32]](#cite_note-36) Fresh from the success of the [Northern Expedition](/wiki/Northern_Expedition "Northern Expedition") to overthrow the warlords, Chiang Kai-shek turned on the communists, who by now numbered in the tens of thousands across China.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFeigon200242-37) Ignoring the orders of the Wuhan-based KMT government, he marched on Shanghai, a city controlled by communist militias. Although the communists welcomed Chiang's arrival, he turned on them, [massacring 5,000](/wiki/Shanghai_massacre_of_1927 "Shanghai massacre of 1927")[[e]](#cite_note-40) with the aid of the [Green Gang](/wiki/Green_Gang "Green Gang").[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFeigon200242-37)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchram1966106-41)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarter197661–62-42) Chiang's army then marched on Wuhan but was prevented from taking the city by CCP General [Ye Ting](/wiki/Ye_Ting "Ye Ting") and his troops.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchram1966112-43) Chiang's allies also attacked communists; for example, in Beijing, Li Dazhao and 19 other leading communists were executed by [Zhang Zuolin](/wiki/Zhang_Zuolin "Zhang Zuolin").[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchram1966106–109-44)[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarter197662-38) Angered by these events, the peasant movement supported by the CCP became more violent. [Ye Dehui](/wiki/Ye_Dehui "Ye Dehui"), a famous scholar, was killed by communists in [Changsha](/wiki/Changsha "Changsha"), and in revenge, KMT general [He Jian](/wiki/He_Jian "He Jian") and his troops gunned down hundreds of peasant militiamen.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchram1966112–113-45) That May, tens of thousands of communists and their sympathizers were killed by KMT troops, with the CCP losing approximately 15,000 of its 25,000 members.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarter197662-38)

### Chinese Civil War and Second Sino-Japanese War

Main articles: [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War") and [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/%E4%B8%AD%E5%9C%8B%E5%B7%A5%E8%BE%B2%E7%B4%85%E8%BB%8D%E8%BB%8D%E6%97%97.svg/250px-%E4%B8%AD%E5%9C%8B%E5%B7%A5%E8%BE%B2%E7%B4%85%E8%BB%8D%E8%BB%8D%E6%97%97.svg.png)](/wiki/File:%E4%B8%AD%E5%9C%8B%E5%B7%A5%E8%BE%B2%E7%B4%85%E8%BB%8D%E8%BB%8D%E6%97%97.svg)

Flag of the [Chinese Workers' and Peasants' Red Army](/wiki/Chinese_Red_Army "Chinese Red Army")

The CCP continued supporting the Wuhan KMT government,[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarter197662-38) but on 15 July 1927 the Wuhan government expelled all communists from the KMT.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarter197663-46) The CCP reacted by founding the [Workers' and Peasants' Red Army of China](/wiki/Chinese_Red_Army "Chinese Red Army"), better known as the "Red Army", to battle the KMT. A battalion led by General [Zhu De](/wiki/Zhu_De "Zhu De") was ordered to take the city of [Nanchang](/wiki/Nanchang "Nanchang") on 1 August 1927 in what became known as the [Nanchang uprising](/wiki/Nanchang_uprising "Nanchang uprising").

Initially successful, Zhu and his troops were forced to retreat after five days, marching south to [Shantou](/wiki/Shantou "Shantou"), and from there being driven into the wilderness of [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian").[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarter197663-46) [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") was appointed commander-in-chief of the Red Army, and led four regiments against Changsha in the [Autumn Harvest Uprising](/wiki/Autumn_Harvest_Uprising "Autumn Harvest Uprising"), hoping to spark peasant uprisings across Hunan.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarter197664-47) His plan was to attack the KMT-held city from three directions on 9 September, but the Fourth Regiment deserted to the KMT cause, attacking the Third Regiment. Mao's army made it to Changsha but could not take it; by 15 September, he accepted defeat, with 1,000 survivors marching east to the [Jinggang Mountains](/wiki/Jinggang_Mountains "Jinggang Mountains") of [Jiangxi](/wiki/Jiangxi "Jiangxi").[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarter197664-47)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchram1966122–125-48)[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFeigon200246–47-49)

The near destruction of the CCP's urban organizational apparatus led to institutional changes within the party.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199272-50) The party adopted [democratic centralism](/wiki/Democratic_centralism "Democratic centralism"), a way to organize revolutionary parties, and established a politburo to function as the standing committee of the central committee.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199272-50) The result was increased centralization of power within the party.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199272-50) At every level of the party this was duplicated, with standing committees now in effective control.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199272-50) The 1929 [Gutian Congress](/wiki/Gutian_Congress "Gutian Congress") was important in establishing the principle of party control over the military, which continues to be a core principle of the party's ideology.[[46]](#cite_note-:Duan-51): 280 After being expelled from the party, Chen Duxiu went on to lead China's [Trotskyist](/wiki/Trotskyism "Trotskyism") movement. [Li Lisan](/wiki/Li_Lisan "Li Lisan") was able to assume *de facto* control of the party organization by 1929–1930.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199272-50)

Li's leadership was a failure, leaving the CCP on the brink of destruction.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199272-50) The Comintern became involved, and by late 1930, his powers had been taken away.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199272-50) By 1935, Mao had become a member of [Politburo Standing Committee of the CCP](/wiki/Politburo_Standing_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Politburo Standing Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") and the party's informal military leader, with [Zhou Enlai](/wiki/Zhou_Enlai "Zhou Enlai") and [Zhang Wentian](/wiki/Zhang_Wentian "Zhang Wentian"), the formal head of the party, serving as his informal deputies.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199272-50) The conflict with the KMT led to the reorganization of the Red Army, with power now centralized in the leadership through the creation of CCP political departments charged with supervising the army.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199272-50)

The [Xi'an Incident](/wiki/Xi%27an_Incident "Xi'an Incident") of December 1936 paused the conflict between the CCP and the KMT.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung1992370-52) Under pressure from Marshal [Zhang Xueliang](/wiki/Zhang_Xueliang "Zhang Xueliang") and the CCP, Chiang Kai-shek finally agreed to a [Second United Front](/wiki/Second_United_Front "Second United Front") focused on repelling the Japanese invaders.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung1992354-53) While the front formally existed until 1945, all collaboration between the two parties had effectively ended by 1940.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung1992354-53) Despite their formal alliance, the CCP used the opportunity to expand and carve out independent bases of operations to prepare for the coming war with the KMT.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung1992355-54) In 1939, the KMT began to restrict CCP expansion within China.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung1992355-54) This led to frequent clashes between CCP and KMT forces[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung1992355-54) which subsided rapidly on the realization on both sides that civil war amidst a foreign invasion was not an option.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung1992355-54) By 1943, the CCP was again actively expanding its territory at the expense of the KMT.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung1992355-54)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Three_Campaigns_of_Chinese_Civil_War.png/330px-Three_Campaigns_of_Chinese_Civil_War.png)](/wiki/File:Three_Campaigns_of_Chinese_Civil_War.png)

Map showing the [Liaoshen](/wiki/Liaoshen_Campaign "Liaoshen Campaign"), [Huaihai](/wiki/Huaihai_campaign "Huaihai campaign"), and [Pingjin Campaigns](/wiki/Pingjin_campaign "Pingjin campaign") that decisively turned the war in favour of the CCP.

Mao Zedong became the Chairman of the [Politburo](/wiki/Politburo_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Politburo of the Chinese Communist Party") in 1943 and the [Chairman of the Central Committee](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Chairman of the Chinese Communist Party") in 1945. After the [Japanese surrender](/wiki/Japanese_surrender "Japanese surrender") in 1945, the war between the CCP and the KMT began again in earnest.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199295-55) The 1945–1949 period had four stages; the first was from August 1945 (when the Japanese surrendered) to June 1946 (when the peace talks between the CCP and the KMT ended).[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199295-55) By 1945, the KMT had three times more soldiers under its command than the CCP and initially appeared to be prevailing.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199295-55) With the cooperation of the US and Japan, the KMT was able to retake major parts of the country.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199295-55) However, KMT rule over the reconquered territories proved unpopular because of its endemic [political corruption](/wiki/Political_corruption "Political corruption").[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199295-55)

Notwithstanding its numerical superiority, the KMT failed to reconquer the rural territories which made up the CCP's stronghold.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199295-55) Around the same time, the CCP launched an invasion of [Manchuria](/wiki/Manchuria "Manchuria"), where they were assisted by the Soviet Union.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199295-55) The second stage, lasting from July 1946 to June 1947, saw the KMT extend its control over major cities such as [Yan'an](/wiki/Yan%27an "Yan'an"), the CCP headquarters, for much of the war.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199295-55) The KMT's successes were hollow; the CCP had tactically withdrawn from the cities, and instead undermined KMT rule there by instigating protests among students and intellectuals. The KMT responded to these demonstrations with heavy-handed repression.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199296-56) In the meantime, the KMT was struggling with factional infighting and Chiang Kai-shek's autocratic control over the party, which weakened its ability to respond to attacks.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199296-56)

The third stage, lasting from July 1947 to August 1948, saw a limited counteroffensive by the CCP.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199296-56) The objective was clearing "Central China, strengthening North China, and recovering Northeast China."[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199696-57) This operation, coupled with military desertions from the KMT, resulted in the KMT losing 2 million of its 3 million troops by the spring of 1948, and saw a significant decline in support for KMT rule.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199296-56) The CCP was consequently able to cut off KMT garrisons in Manchuria and retake several territories.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199696-57)

The last stage, lasting from September 1948 to December 1949, saw the communists go on the offensive and the collapse of KMT rule in mainland China as a whole.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199696-57) Mao's [proclamation of the founding of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Proclamation of the People's Republic of China") on 1 October 1949 marked the end of the second phase of the Chinese Civil War (or the [Chinese Communist Revolution](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Revolution "Chinese Communist Revolution"), as it is called by the CCP).[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeung199696-57)

### Proclamation of the PRC and the Mao era

Further information: [Proclamation of the People's Republic of China](/wiki/Proclamation_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Proclamation of the People's Republic of China")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Stalin_birthday2.jpg/250px-Stalin_birthday2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stalin_birthday2.jpg)

Chinese communists celebrate Joseph Stalin's birthday, 1949.

Mao proclaimed the founding of the People's Republic of China (PRC) before a massive crowd at Tiananmen Square on 1 October 1949. The CCP headed the [Central People's Government](/wiki/Central_People%27s_Government_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China_(1949%E2%80%931954) "Central People's Government of the People's Republic of China (1949–1954)").[[4]](#cite_note-auto-7): 118 From this time through the 1980s, top leaders of the CCP (such as Mao Zedong, Lin Biao, Zhou Enlai and Deng Xiaoping) were largely the same military leaders prior to the PRC's founding.[[53]](#cite_note-:2-58) As a result, informal personal ties between political and military leaders dominated civil-military relations.[[53]](#cite_note-:2-58)

Stalin proposed a one-party constitution when [Liu Shaoqi](/wiki/Liu_Shaoqi "Liu Shaoqi") visited the Soviet Union in 1952.[[54]](#cite_note-59) The constitution of the PRC in 1954 subsequently abolished the previous coalition government and established the CCP's one-party system.[[55]](#cite_note-60)[[56]](#cite_note-61) In 1957, the CCP launched the [Anti-Rightist Campaign](/wiki/Anti-Rightist_Campaign "Anti-Rightist Campaign") against political dissidents and prominent figures from minor parties, which resulted in the political persecution of at least 550,000 people. The campaign significantly damaged the limited pluralistic nature in the socialist republic and solidified the country's status as a [one-party state](/wiki/One-party_state "One-party state").[[57]](#cite_note-62)[[58]](#cite_note-63)

The Anti-Rightist Campaign led to the catastrophic results of the [Second Five Year Plan](/wiki/Second_five-year_plan_(China) "Second five-year plan (China)") from 1958 to 1962, known as the [Great Leap Forward](/wiki/Great_Leap_Forward "Great Leap Forward"). In an effort to transform the country from an agrarian economy into an industrialized one, the CCP collectivized farmland, formed people's communes, and diverted labour to factories. General mismanagement and exaggerations of harvests by CCP officials led to the [Great Chinese Famine](/wiki/Great_Chinese_Famine "Great Chinese Famine"), which resulted in an estimated 15 to 45 million deaths,[[59]](#cite_note-64)[[60]](#cite_note-65) making it the largest famine in recorded history.[[61]](#cite_note-66)[[62]](#cite_note-67)[[63]](#cite_note-68) Defense Minister [Peng Dehuai](/wiki/Peng_Dehuai "Peng Dehuai"), who criticized Mao for his role in causing the disaster, was purged for this in 1959.

#### Sino-Soviet split and Cultural Revolution

Main articles: [Sino-Soviet split](/wiki/Sino-Soviet_split "Sino-Soviet split") and [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution")

During the 1960s and 1970s, the CCP experienced a significant ideological separation from the Communist Party of the Soviet Union which had gone through a period of "[de-Stalinization](/wiki/De-Stalinization "De-Stalinization")" under [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev").[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKornbergFaust2005103-69) By that time, Mao had begun saying that the "continued revolution under the [dictatorship of the proletariat](/wiki/Dictatorship_of_the_proletariat "Dictatorship of the proletariat")" stipulated that class enemies continued to exist even though the socialist revolution seemed to be complete, leading to the [Cultural Revolution](/wiki/Cultural_Revolution "Cultural Revolution") in which millions were persecuted and killed.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWong2005131-70) During the Cultural Revolution, party leaders such as Liu Shaoqi, [Deng Xiaoping](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping "Deng Xiaoping"), and [He Long](/wiki/He_Long "He Long") were purged or exiled, and the [Gang of Four](/wiki/Gang_of_Four "Gang of Four"), led by Mao's wife [Jiang Qing](/wiki/Jiang_Qing "Jiang Qing"), emerged to fill in the power vacuum left behind.

### Reform and opening up

Main article: [Reform and opening up](/wiki/Reform_and_opening_up "Reform and opening up")

Following Mao's death in 1976, CCP chairman [Hua Guofeng](/wiki/Hua_Guofeng "Hua Guofeng") [removed the Gang of Four](/wiki/Smashing_the_Gang_of_Four "Smashing the Gang of Four") from power and began rehabilitating party leaders persecuted during the Cultural Revolution including Deng Xiaoping. A power struggle between Hua and Deng erupted.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWong200547-71) Deng won the struggle, and became China's paramount leader in 1978.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWong200547-71) Deng, alongside [Hu Yaobang](/wiki/Hu_Yaobang "Hu Yaobang") and [Zhao Ziyang](/wiki/Zhao_Ziyang "Zhao Ziyang"), spearheaded the [reform and opening up](/wiki/Reform_and_opening_up "Reform and opening up") policies, and introduced the ideological concept of [socialism with Chinese characteristics](/wiki/Socialism_with_Chinese_characteristics "Socialism with Chinese characteristics"), opening China to the world's markets.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012254-72) In reversing some of Mao's "leftist" policies, Deng argued that a socialist state could use the [market economy](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy") without itself being capitalist.[[68]](#cite_note-marketvsplanning-73) While asserting the political power of the CCP, the change in policy generated significant economic growth.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This was justified on the basis that "[Practice is the Sole Criterion for the Truth](/wiki/1978_Truth_Criterion_Controversy "1978 Truth Criterion Controversy")", a principle reinforced through a 1978 article that aimed to combat dogmatism and criticized the "[Two Whatevers](/wiki/Two_Whatevers "Two Whatevers")" policy.[[69]](#cite_note-74)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

The new ideology, however, was contested on both sides of the spectrum, by Maoists to the left of the CCP's leadership, as well as by those supporting political liberalization. In 1981, the Party adopted a [historical resolution](/wiki/Resolution_on_Certain_Questions_in_the_History_of_Our_Party_since_the_Founding_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Resolution on Certain Questions in the History of Our Party since the Founding of the People's Republic of China"), which assessed the historical legacy of the Mao Zedong era and the future priorities of the CCP.[[70]](#cite_note-:22-75): 6 With other social factors, the conflicts culminated in the [1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre](/wiki/1989_Tiananmen_Square_protests_and_massacre "1989 Tiananmen Square protests and massacre").[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan201225-76) The protests having been crushed and the reformist party general secretary Zhao Ziyang under house arrest, Deng's economic policies resumed and by the early 1990s the concept of a [socialist market economy](/wiki/Socialist_market_economy "Socialist market economy") had been introduced.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVogel2011682-77) In 1997, Deng's beliefs (officially called "[Deng Xiaoping Theory](/wiki/Deng_Xiaoping_Theory "Deng Xiaoping Theory")") were embedded into the [CCP's constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Constitution of the Chinese Communist Party").[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVogel2011684-78)

CCP general secretary [Jiang Zemin](/wiki/Jiang_Zemin "Jiang Zemin") succeeded Deng as paramount leader in 1989 and continued most of his policies.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012100-79) In the 1990s, the CCP transformed from a veteran revolutionary leadership that was both leading militarily and politically, to a political elite increasingly renewed according to institutionalized norms in the civil bureaucracy.[[53]](#cite_note-:2-58) Leadership was largely selected based on rules and norms on promotion and retirement, educational background, and managerial and technical expertise.[[53]](#cite_note-:2-58) There is a largely separate group of professionalized military officers, serving under top CCP leadership largely through formal relationships within institutional channels.[[53]](#cite_note-:2-58)

The CCP ratified Jiang's [Three Represents](/wiki/Three_Represents "Three Represents") concept for the 2003 revision of the party's constitution, as a "guiding ideology" to encourage the party to represent "advanced productive forces, the progressive course of China's culture, and the fundamental interests of the people."[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012238-80) The theory legitimized the entry of private business owners and [bourgeois](/wiki/Bourgeois "Bourgeois") elements into the party.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012238-80) [Hu Jintao](/wiki/Hu_Jintao "Hu Jintao"), Jiang Zemin's successor as general secretary, took office in 2002.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012317-81) Unlike Mao, Deng and Jiang Zemin, Hu laid emphasis on [collective leadership](/wiki/Collective_leadership "Collective leadership") and opposed one-man dominance of the political system.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012317-81) The insistence on focusing on economic growth led to a [wide range of serious social problems](/wiki/Social_issues_in_China "Social issues in China"). To address these, Hu introduced two main ideological concepts: the [Scientific Outlook on Development](/wiki/Scientific_Outlook_on_Development "Scientific Outlook on Development") and [Harmonious Society](/wiki/Harmonious_Society "Harmonious Society").[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012329-82) Hu resigned from his post as the CCP general secretary and Chairman of the CMC at the [18th National Congress](/wiki/18th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "18th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") held in 2012, and was succeeded in both posts by Xi Jinping.[[78]](#cite_note-83)[[79]](#cite_note-84)

### Xi Jinping and the new era

Main article: [General secretaryship of Xi Jinping](/wiki/General_secretaryship_of_Xi_Jinping "General secretaryship of Xi Jinping")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/%E5%BA%86%E7%A5%9D%E4%B8%AD%E5%9B%BD%E5%85%B1%E4%BA%A7%E5%85%9A%E6%88%90%E7%AB%8B100%E5%91%A8%E5%B9%B4%E5%A4%A7%E4%BC%9A_%E5%A4%A9%E5%AE%89%E9%97%A8%E5%B9%BF%E5%9C%BA_%E5%B8%83%E6%99%AF.jpg/250px-%E5%BA%86%E7%A5%9D%E4%B8%AD%E5%9B%BD%E5%85%B1%E4%BA%A7%E5%85%9A%E6%88%90%E7%AB%8B100%E5%91%A8%E5%B9%B4%E5%A4%A7%E4%BC%9A_%E5%A4%A9%E5%AE%89%E9%97%A8%E5%B9%BF%E5%9C%BA_%E5%B8%83%E6%99%AF.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E5%BA%86%E7%A5%9D%E4%B8%AD%E5%9B%BD%E5%85%B1%E4%BA%A7%E5%85%9A%E6%88%90%E7%AB%8B100%E5%91%A8%E5%B9%B4%E5%A4%A7%E4%BC%9A_%E5%A4%A9%E5%AE%89%E9%97%A8%E5%B9%BF%E5%9C%BA_%E5%B8%83%E6%99%AF.jpg)

The scenery of decorations at Tiananmen Square on 30 June 2021 to celebrate the 100th anniversary of the CCP's founding

Since taking power of the general secretary in 2012, Xi Jinping has initiated [a wide-reaching anti-corruption campaign](/wiki/Anti-corruption_campaign_under_Xi_Jinping "Anti-corruption campaign under Xi Jinping"), while centralizing powers in the office of CCP general secretary at the expense of the collective leadership of prior decades.[[80]](#cite_note-85) Commentators have described the campaign as a defining part of [Xi's general secretaryship](/wiki/General_secretaryship_of_Xi_Jinping "General secretaryship of Xi Jinping") as well as "the principal reason why he has been able to consolidate his power so quickly and effectively."[[81]](#cite_note-86) Xi's leadership has also overseen an increase in the Party's role in China.[[82]](#cite_note-87) Since 2014, the CCP has led efforts in Xinjiang that involve the detention of more than 1 million Uyghurs and other ethnic minorities in [internment camps](/wiki/Xinjiang_internment_camps "Xinjiang internment camps"), as well as [persecution](/wiki/Persecution_of_Uyghurs_in_China "Persecution of Uyghurs in China") that some characterized as a [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide") or [crimes against humanity](/wiki/Crimes_against_humanity "Crimes against humanity").[[83]](#cite_note-88)

Xi has added [his ideology](/wiki/Xi_Jinping_Thought "Xi Jinping Thought"), named after himself, into the CCP constitution in 2017.[[84]](#cite_note-:0-89) The National Congress of the CCP declared that China and the CCP entered a "new era of socialism with Chinese characteristics" in 2012.[[85]](#cite_note-90)[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*] Celebrations of the [100th anniversary of the CCP's founding](/wiki/100th_Anniversary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "100th Anniversary of the Chinese Communist Party"), one of the [Two Centenaries](/wiki/Two_Centenaries "Two Centenaries"), took place on 1 July 2021.[[86]](#cite_note-91) In the sixth plenary session of the 19th Central Committee in November 2021, the CCP adopted a [resolution on the Party's history](/wiki/Resolution_on_the_Major_Achievements_and_Historical_Experience_of_the_Party_over_the_Past_Century "Resolution on the Major Achievements and Historical Experience of the Party over the Past Century"), which for the first time credited Xi as being the "main innovator" of Xi Jinping Thought while also declaring Xi's leadership as being "the key to the [great rejuvenation of the Chinese nation](/wiki/Great_rejuvenation_of_the_Chinese_nation "Great rejuvenation of the Chinese nation")".[[87]](#cite_note-92)[[88]](#cite_note-93) In comparison with the other historical resolutions, Xi's one did not herald a major change in how the CCP evaluated its history.[[89]](#cite_note-94) After the [20th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/20th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "20th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") held in 2022, Xi Jinping was [re-elected](/wiki/First_plenary_session_of_the_20th_Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "First plenary session of the 20th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") as the CCP general secretary for a third term, that made Xi the first [CCP leader](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Leader of the Chinese Communist Party") since Mao Zedong to be chosen for a third term.[[90]](#cite_note-95)[[91]](#cite_note-96)
