Main article: [Ideology of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Ideology_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Ideology of the Chinese Communist Party")

### Formal ideology

Further information: [Generations of Chinese leadership](/wiki/Generations_of_Chinese_leadership "Generations of Chinese leadership")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Marx_et_Engels_%C3%A0_Shanghai.jpg/250px-Marx_et_Engels_%C3%A0_Shanghai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marx_et_Engels_%C3%A0_Shanghai.jpg)

A monument dedicated to Karl Marx (left) and Friedrich Engels (right) in Shanghai

The core ideology of the party has evolved with each distinct [generation of Chinese leadership](/wiki/Generations_of_Chinese_leadership "Generations of Chinese leadership"). As both the CCP and the [People's Liberation Army](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Army "People's Liberation Army") promote their members according to seniority, it is possible to discern distinct generations of Chinese leadership.[[92]](#cite_note-auto1-97)

[Marxism–Leninism](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism") was the first official ideology of the CCP.[[93]](#cite_note-MLT-98) According to the CCP, "Marxism–Leninism reveals the [universal laws governing the development of history of human society](/wiki/Historical_materialism "Historical materialism")."[[93]](#cite_note-MLT-98) To the CCP, Marxism–Leninism provides a "vision of the [contradictions in capitalist society](/wiki/Capitalist_mode_of_production_(Marxist_theory) "Capitalist mode of production (Marxist theory)") and of the inevitability of a future socialist and communist societies".[[93]](#cite_note-MLT-98) According to the *[People's Daily](/wiki/People%27s_Daily "People's Daily")*, [Mao Zedong Thought](/wiki/Mao_Zedong_Thought "Mao Zedong Thought") "is Marxism–Leninism applied and developed in China".[[93]](#cite_note-MLT-98) Mao Zedong Thought was conceived not only by Mao Zedong, but by leading party officials, according to [Xinhua News Agency](/wiki/Xinhua_News_Agency "Xinhua News Agency").[[94]](#cite_note-MaoZedongThought-99)

Deng Xiaoping Theory was added to the party constitution at the [14th National Congress](/wiki/14th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "14th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") in 1992.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVogel2011684-78) The concepts of "socialism with Chinese characteristics" and "the primary stage of socialism" were credited to the theory.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVogel2011684-78) Deng Xiaoping Theory can be defined as a belief that [state socialism](/wiki/State_socialism "State socialism") and [state planning](/wiki/Planned_economy "Planned economy") is not by definition communist, and that market mechanisms are class neutral.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVogel2011668-100) In addition, the party needs to react to the changing situation dynamically; to know if a certain policy is obsolete or not, the party had to "[seek truth from facts](/wiki/Seek_truth_from_facts "Seek truth from facts")" and follow the slogan "practice is the sole criterion for the truth".[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChan2003180-101) At the 14th National Congress, Jiang reiterated Deng's mantra that it was unnecessary to ask if something was socialist or capitalist, since the important factor was whether it worked.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVogel2011685-102) The CCP's ideology today is often summarized as socialism with Chinese characteristics.[[98]](#cite_note-103)

The "Three Represents", Jiang Zemin's contribution to the party's ideology, was adopted by the party at the [16th National Congress](/wiki/16th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "16th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party"). The Three Represents defines the role of the CCP, and stresses that the Party must always represent the requirements for developing China's advanced productive forces, the orientation of China's advanced culture and the fundamental interests of the overwhelming majority of the Chinese people."[[99]](#cite_note-104)[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChan2003201-105) Certain segments within the CCP criticized the Three Represents as being un-Marxist and a betrayal of basic Marxist values. Supporters viewed it as a further development of socialism with Chinese characteristics.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuhn2011108–109-106) Jiang disagreed, and had concluded that attaining the communist mode of production, as formulated by earlier communists, was more complex than had been realized, and that it was useless to try to force a change in the mode of production, as it had to develop naturally, by following the "[economic laws of history](/wiki/Historical_materialism "Historical materialism")".[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuhn2011107–108-107) The theory is most notable for allowing capitalists, officially referred to as the "new social strata", to join the party on the grounds that they engaged in "honest labor and work" and through their labour contributed "to build[ing] socialism with Chinese characteristics."[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuhn2011110-108)

In 2003, the 3rd Plenary Session of the [16th Central Committee](/wiki/16th_Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "16th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party") conceived and formulated the ideology of the [Scientific Outlook on Development](/wiki/Scientific_Outlook_on_Development "Scientific Outlook on Development") (SOD).[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIzuhara2013110-109) It is considered to be Hu Jintao's contribution to the official ideological discourse.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuoGuo2008119-110) The SOD incorporates [scientific socialism](/wiki/Scientific_socialism "Scientific socialism"), [sustainable development](/wiki/Sustainable_development "Sustainable development"), [social welfare](/wiki/Social_welfare "Social welfare"), a [humanistic](/wiki/Humanism "Humanism") society, increased democracy, and, ultimately, the creation of a [Socialist Harmonious Society](/wiki/Socialist_Harmonious_Society "Socialist Harmonious Society"). According to official statements by the CCP, the concept integrates "Marxism with the reality of contemporary China and with the underlying features of our times, and it fully embodies the Marxist worldview on and methodology for development."[[106]](#cite_note-111) In 2012, Xi and CCP ideologues coined the phrase "[Chinese Dream](/wiki/Chinese_Dream "Chinese Dream")" to describe his overarching plans for China as its leader. Xi has linked the "Chinese Dream" with the phrase "great rejuvenation of the Chinese nation,"[[107]](#cite_note-112) an overarching goal by 2049, the 100th anniversary of the People's Republic of China.[[108]](#cite_note-:13-113) [Xi Jinping Thought on Socialism with Chinese Characteristics for a New Era](/wiki/Xi_Jinping_Thought "Xi Jinping Thought"), commonly known as Xi Jinping Thought, was added to the party constitution in the [19th National Congress](/wiki/19th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "19th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") in 2017.[[84]](#cite_note-:0-89)[[109]](#cite_note-114)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/A_political_slogan_on_the_wall_in_Longhua_District%2C_Shenzhen%2C_Guangdong%2C_China%2C_picture1.jpg/250px-A_political_slogan_on_the_wall_in_Longhua_District%2C_Shenzhen%2C_Guangdong%2C_China%2C_picture1.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_political_slogan_on_the_wall_in_Longhua_District,_Shenzhen,_Guangdong,_China,_picture1.jpg)

A billboard advertising Xi Jinping Thought in [Shenzhen](/wiki/Shenzhen "Shenzhen"), Guangdong

The party combines elements of both [socialist patriotism](/wiki/Socialist_patriotism "Socialist patriotism")[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZhao200428-115)[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGhaiArupChanock200077-116) and [Chinese nationalism](/wiki/Chinese_nationalism "Chinese nationalism").[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZheng2012119-117) As Deng put it, "Some have said that not loving Socialism is not the same as not loving one's motherland. Is the motherland something abstract? If you do not love socialist New China led by the Communist Party, what motherland do you love?"[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDing2006144-118) According to official pronouncements, the CCP was the best representative of the nation, communists were the most devoted patriots, and socialism the only viable road for China to become "a great nation".[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDing2006144-118) Deng Liqun, in a similar vein, said, "One cannot demonstrate that one loves the motherland if one shows no deep love for the socialist system and the Communist Party. In short, in our times, loving the Chinese Communist Party is the highest expression of Chinese patriotism."[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDing2006145-119) The CCP promotes [Chinese unification](/wiki/Chinese_unification "Chinese unification") and opposes [Taiwan independence](/wiki/Taiwan_independence_movement "Taiwan independence movement").[[115]](#cite_note-:6-120)

### Economics

Deng did not believe that the fundamental difference between the capitalist mode of production and the socialist mode of production was [central planning](/wiki/Central_planning "Central planning") versus [free markets](/wiki/Free_market "Free market"). He said, "A planned economy is not the definition of socialism, because there is planning under capitalism; the market economy happens under socialism, too. Planning and market forces are both ways of controlling economic activity".[[68]](#cite_note-marketvsplanning-73) Jiang Zemin supported Deng's thinking, and stated in a party gathering that it did not matter if a certain mechanism was capitalist or socialist, because the only thing that mattered was whether it worked.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVogel2011682-77) It was at this gathering that Jiang Zemin introduced the term socialist market economy, which replaced [Chen Yun](/wiki/Chen_Yun "Chen Yun")'s "planned socialist market economy".[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVogel2011682-77) In his report to the 14th National Congress Jiang Zemin told the delegates that the socialist state would "let market forces play a basic role in resource allocation."[[116]](#cite_note-basicdecisive-121) At the 15th National Congress, the party line was changed to "make market forces further play their role in resource allocation"; this line continued until the 3rd Plenary Session of the [18th Central Committee](/wiki/18th_Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "18th Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party"),[[116]](#cite_note-basicdecisive-121) when it was amended to "let market forces play a *decisive* role in [resource allocation](/wiki/Resource_allocation "Resource allocation")."[[116]](#cite_note-basicdecisive-121) Despite this, the 3rd Plenary Session of the 18th Central Committee upheld the creed "Maintain the dominance of the [public sector](/wiki/Public_sector "Public sector") and strengthen the economic vitality of the state-owned economy."[[116]](#cite_note-basicdecisive-121)

> "... their theory that capitalism is the ultimate [force] has been shaken, and socialist development has experienced a miracle. Western capitalism has suffered reversals, a financial crisis, a credit crisis, a crisis of confidence, and their self-conviction has wavered. Western countries have begun to reflect, and openly or secretively compare themselves against China's politics, economy and path."

— [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping"), on the inevitability of socialism[[117]](#cite_note-122)

The CCP views the world as organized into two opposing camps; socialist and capitalist.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeazleKnight200762-123) They insist that socialism, on the basis of [historical materialism](/wiki/Historical_materialism "Historical materialism"), will eventually triumph over capitalism.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeazleKnight200762-123) In recent years, when the party has been asked to explain the capitalist [globalization](/wiki/Globalization "Globalization") occurring, the party has returned to the writings of [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx").[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeazleKnight200762-123) Despite admitting that globalization developed through the capitalist system, the party's leaders and theorists argue that globalization is not intrinsically capitalist.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeazleKnight200763-124) The reason being that if globalization was purely capitalist, it would exclude an alternative socialist form of modernity.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeazleKnight200763-124) Globalization, as with the market economy, therefore does not have one specific class character (neither socialist nor capitalist) according to the party.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeazleKnight200763-124) The insistence that globalization is not fixed in nature comes from Deng's insistence that China can pursue socialist modernization by incorporating elements of capitalism.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeazleKnight200763-124) Because of this there is considerable optimism within the CCP that despite the current capitalist dominance of globalization, globalization can be turned into a vehicle supporting socialism.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeazleKnight200764-125)

### Analysis

While foreign analysts generally agree that the CCP has rejected orthodox Marxism–Leninism and Mao Zedong Thought (or at least basic thoughts within orthodox thinking), the CCP itself disagrees.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh2008104-126) Critics of the CCP argue that Jiang Zemin ended the party's formal commitment to Marxism–Leninism with the introduction of the ideological theory, the Three Represents.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuhn201199-127) However, party theorist [Leng Rong](/wiki/Leng_Rong "Leng Rong") disagrees, claiming that "Jiang rid the Party of the ideological obstacles to different kinds of ownership ... He did not give up Marxism or socialism. He strengthened the Party by providing a modern understanding of Marxism and socialism—which is why we talk about a 'socialist market economy' with Chinese characteristics."[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuhn201199-127) The attainment of true "communism" is still described as the CCP's and China's "ultimate goal".[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuhn2011527-128) While the CCP claims that China is in the [primary stage of socialism](/wiki/Primary_stage_of_socialism "Primary stage of socialism"), party theorists argue that the current development stage "looks a lot like capitalism".[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuhn2011527-128) Alternatively, certain party theorists argue that "capitalism is the early or first stage of communism."[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuhn2011527-128) Some have dismissed the concept of a primary stage of socialism as intellectual cynicism.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuhn2011527-128) For example, [Robert Lawrence Kuhn](/wiki/Robert_Lawrence_Kuhn "Robert Lawrence Kuhn"), a former foreign adviser to the Chinese government, stated: "When I first heard this rationale, I thought it more comic than clever—a wry caricature of hack propagandists leaked by intellectual cynics. But the 100-year horizon comes from serious political theorists."[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKuhn2011527-128)

American political scientist and [sinologist](/wiki/Sinology "Sinology") [David Shambaugh](/wiki/David_Shambaugh "David Shambaugh") argues that before the "[Practice Is the Sole Criterion for the Truth](/wiki/1978_Truth_Criterion_Controversy "1978 Truth Criterion Controversy")" campaign, the relationship between ideology and decision making was a deductive one, meaning that policy-making was derived from ideological knowledge.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh2008105-129) However, under Deng's leadership this relationship was turned upside down, with decision making justifying ideology.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh2008105-129) Chinese policy-makers have described the [Soviet Union's state ideology](/wiki/Ideology_of_the_Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Ideology of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union") as "rigid, unimaginative, ossified, and disconnected from reality", believing that this was one of the reasons for the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union"). Therefore, Shambaugh argues, Chinese policy-makers believe that their party ideology must be dynamic to safeguard the party's rule.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh2008105-129)

British sinologist [Kerry Brown](/wiki/Kerry_Brown_(historian) "Kerry Brown (historian)") argues that the CCP does not have an ideology, and that the party organization is [pragmatic](/wiki/Pragmatism "Pragmatism") and interested only in what works.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown201252-130) The party itself argues against this assertion. Hu Jintao stated in 2012 that the Western world is "threatening to divide us" and that "the international culture of the West is strong while we are weak ... Ideological and cultural fields are our main targets".[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown201252-130) As such, the CCP puts a great deal of effort into the party schools and into crafting its ideological message.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown201252-130)

## Governance

See also: [Leader of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Leader of the Chinese Communist Party")

### Collective leadership

Further information: [Collective leadership](/wiki/Collective_leadership "Collective leadership")

Collective leadership, the idea that decisions will be taken through consensus, has been the ideal in the CCP.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUnger200222-131) The concept has its origins back to Lenin and the Russian Bolshevik Party.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaylis1989102-132) At the level of the central party leadership this means that, for instance, all members of the Politburo Standing Committee are of equal standing (each member having only one vote).[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUnger200222-131) A member of the Politburo Standing Committee often represents a sector; during Mao's reign, he controlled the People's Liberation Army, [Kang Sheng](/wiki/Kang_Sheng "Kang Sheng"), the security apparatus, and Zhou Enlai, the [State Council](/wiki/State_Council_of_China "State Council of China") and the [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(China) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (China)").[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUnger200222-131) This counts as informal power.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUnger200222-131) Despite this, in a paradoxical relation, members of a body are ranked [hierarchically](/wiki/Hierarchy "Hierarchy") (despite the fact that members are in theory equal to one another).[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUnger200222-131) Informally, the collective leadership is headed by a "[leadership core](/wiki/Leadership_core "Leadership core")"; that is, the [paramount leader](/wiki/Paramount_leader "Paramount leader"), the person who holds the offices of CCP general secretary, [CMC chairman](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Central_Military_Commission_(China) "Chairman of the Central Military Commission (China)") and [PRC president](/wiki/President_of_China "President of China").[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUnger200222–24-133)[[129]](#cite_note-134) Before Jiang Zemin's tenure as paramount leader, the party core and collective leadership were indistinguishable.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUnger2002158-135) In practice, the core was not responsible to the collective leadership.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUnger2002158-135) However, by the time of Jiang, the party had begun propagating a responsibility system, referring to it in official pronouncements as the "core of the collective leadership".[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUnger2002158-135) Academics have noted a decline in collective leadership under Xi Jinping.[[131]](#cite_note-136)[[132]](#cite_note-137)[[133]](#cite_note-138)

### Democratic centralism

This paragraph is an excerpt from [Democratic centralism](/wiki/Democratic_centralism "Democratic centralism").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Democratic_centralism&action=edit)]

[Democratic centralism](/wiki/Democratic_centralism "Democratic centralism") is a [Leninist](/wiki/Leninist "Leninist") principle adopted by [communist states](/wiki/Communist_state "Communist state") and their ruling [communist parties](/wiki/Communist_party "Communist party"). It has also been adopted as the organizational principle of non-ruling communist parties, [transmission belt](/wiki/Transmission_belt "Transmission belt") organizations, and other units operating within communist state systems, as well as by some non-communist organizations. Democratic centralism combines structured participation and consultation within a unified system of organs, so that decisions, once debated and adopted, are then meant to be implemented uniformly throughout the organization.

### Supervision

The CCP conducts internal supervision through its [Central Commission for Discipline Inspection](/wiki/Central_Commission_for_Discipline_Inspection "Central Commission for Discipline Inspection") (CCDI). *Shuanggui* was an intra-party disciplinary process conducted by the CCDI. The process, which literally translates to "double regulation", aims to extract confessions from members accused of violating party rules. According to the Dui Hua Foundation, tactics such as cigarette burns, beatings and simulated drowning are among those used to extract confessions. Other reported techniques include the use of induced hallucinations, with one subject of this method reporting that "In the end I was so exhausted, I agreed to all the accusations against me even though they were false."[[134]](#cite_note-139) In 2018, the *shuanggui* process was superseded by *[liuzhi](/wiki/Liuzhi_(supervision) "Liuzhi (supervision)")* or "retention in custody", which expands beyond CCP members to the entire public sector, academics, and business leaders.[[135]](#cite_note-:05-140)[[136]](#cite_note-141)

### United front

Further information: [United front (China)](/wiki/United_front_(China) "United front (China)"), [First United Front](/wiki/First_United_Front "First United Front"), and [Second United Front](/wiki/Second_United_Front "Second United Front")

The CCP employs a political strategy that it terms "united front work" that involves groups and key individuals that are influenced or controlled by the CCP and used to advance its interests.[[137]](#cite_note-:02-142)[[138]](#cite_note-143) United front work is managed primarily but not exclusively by the [United Front Work Department](/wiki/United_Front_Work_Department "United Front Work Department") (UFWD).[[139]](#cite_note-144) The united front has historically been a [popular front](/wiki/Popular_front "Popular front") that has included eight [legally permitted political parties](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_China "List of political parties in China") alongside other [people's organizations](/wiki/People%27s_organization "People's organization") which have nominal representation in the [National People's Congress](/wiki/National_People%27s_Congress "National People's Congress") and the [Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference](/wiki/Chinese_People%27s_Political_Consultative_Conference "Chinese People's Political Consultative Conference") (CPPCC).[[140]](#cite_note-145) However, the CPPCC is a body without real power.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackerrasMcMillenWatson200170-146) While consultation does take place, it is supervised and directed by the CCP.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackerrasMcMillenWatson200170-146) Under Xi Jinping, the united front and its targets of [influence](/wiki/Influence_operations "Influence operations") have expanded in size and scope.[[142]](#cite_note-147)[[143]](#cite_note-:32-148)

## Organization

Main articles: [Organization of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Organization_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Organization of the Chinese Communist Party") and [Cadre system of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Cadre_system_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Cadre system of the Chinese Communist Party")

### Central organization

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/18th_National_Congress_of_the_Communist_Party_of_China.jpg/250px-18th_National_Congress_of_the_Communist_Party_of_China.jpg)](/wiki/File:18th_National_Congress_of_the_Communist_Party_of_China.jpg)

The [18th National Congress](/wiki/18th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "18th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party"), convened in November 2012

The [National Congress](/wiki/National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") is the party's highest body, and, since the [9th National Congress](/wiki/9th_National_Congress_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "9th National Congress of the Chinese Communist Party") in 1969, has been convened every five years (prior to the 9th Congress they were convened on an irregular basis). According to the party's constitution, a congress may not be postponed except "under extraordinary circumstances".[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackerrasMcMillenWatson2001228-149) The party constitution gives the National Congress six responsibilities:[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackerrasMcMillenWatson2001229-150)

1. Electing the [Central Committee](/wiki/Central_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Central Committee of the Chinese Communist Party");
2. Electing the [Central Commission for Discipline Inspection](/wiki/Central_Commission_for_Discipline_Inspection "Central Commission for Discipline Inspection") (CCDI);
3. Examining the report of the outgoing Central Committee;
4. Examining the report of the outgoing CCDI;
5. Discussing and enacting party policies; and,
6. Revising the party's constitution.

In practice, the delegates rarely discuss issues at length at the National Congresses.[[146]](#cite_note-151)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Most substantive discussion takes place before the congress, in the preparation period, among a group of top party leaders.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackerrasMcMillenWatson2001229-150) In between National Congresses, the Central Committee is the highest decision-making institution.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackerrasMcMillenWatson200166-152) The CCDI is responsible for supervising party's internal anti-corruption and ethics system.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJoseph2010394-153) In between congresses the CCDI is under the authority of the Central Committee.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJoseph2010394-153)
The Central Committee, as the party's highest decision-making institution between national congresses, elects several bodies to carry out its work.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiu201141-154) The first [plenary session](/wiki/Plenary_session "Plenary session") of a newly elected central committee elects the [general secretary of the Central Committee](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party"), the [party's leader](/wiki/Leader_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Leader of the Chinese Communist Party"); the [Central Military Commission](/wiki/Central_Military_Commission_(China) "Central Military Commission (China)") (CMC); the [Politburo](/wiki/Politburo_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Politburo of the Chinese Communist Party"); the Politburo Standing Committee (PSC). The first plenum also endorses the composition of the [Secretariat](/wiki/Secretariat_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Secretariat of the Chinese Communist Party") and the leadership of the CCDI.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELiu201141-154) According to the party constitution, the general secretary must be a member of the Politburo Standing Committee (PSC), and is responsible for convening meetings of the PSC and the Politburo, while also presiding over the work of the Secretariat.[[150]](#cite_note-CRI-155) The Politburo "exercises the functions and powers of the Central Committee when a plenum is not in session".[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackerrasMcMillenWatson200185-156) The PSC is the party's highest decision-making institution when the Politburo, the Central Committee and the National Congress are not in session.[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiller20117-157) It convenes at least once a week.[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJoseph2010169-158) It was established at the 8th National Congress, in 1958, to take over the policy-making role formerly assumed by the Secretariat.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi200964-159) The Secretariat is the top implementation body of the Central Committee, and can make decisions within the policy framework established by the Politburo; it is also responsible for supervising the work of organizations that report directly into the Central Committee, for example departments, commissions, publications, and so on.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFu1993201-160) The CMC is the highest decision-making institution on military affairs within the party, and controls the operations of the People's Liberation Army.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackerrasMcMillenWatson200174-161) The general secretary has, since Jiang Zemin, also served as Chairman of the CMC.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackerrasMcMillenWatson200174-161) Unlike the collective leadership ideal of other party organs, the CMC chairman acts as the [supreme commander](/wiki/Supreme_command_of_the_armed_forces_in_China "Supreme command of the armed forces in China") with full authority to appoint or dismiss top military officers at will.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackerrasMcMillenWatson200174-161)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Front_cover_of_Constitution_of_the_Communist_Party_of_China.jpg/250px-Front_cover_of_Constitution_of_the_Communist_Party_of_China.jpg)](/wiki/File:Front_cover_of_Constitution_of_the_Communist_Party_of_China.jpg)

Front cover of the Constitution of the Chinese Communist Party

A first plenum of the Central Committee also elects heads of departments, bureaus, central leading groups and other institutions to pursue its work during a term (a "term" being the period elapsing between national congresses, usually five years).[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackerrasMcMillenWatson2001228-149) The [General Office](/wiki/General_Office_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Office of the Chinese Communist Party") is the party's "nerve centre", in charge of day-to-day administrative work, including communications, protocol, and setting agendas for meetings.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012212-162) The CCP currently has six main central departments: the [Organization Department](/wiki/Organization_Department_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Organization Department of the Chinese Communist Party"), responsible for overseeing provincial appointments and vetting cadres for future appointments,[[158]](#cite_note-COD-163) the [Publicity Department](/wiki/Publicity_Department_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Publicity Department of the Chinese Communist Party") (formerly "Propaganda Department"), which oversees the media and formulates the party line to the media,[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcGregor201217-164)[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuo2012123-165) the United Front Work Department, which oversees the country's eight minor parties, [people's organizations](/wiki/People%27s_organization "People's organization"), and influence groups inside and outside of the country,[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmithWest2012127-166) the [International Department](/wiki/International_Department_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "International Department of the Chinese Communist Party"), functioning as the party's "foreign affairs ministry" with other parties, the [Society Work Department](/wiki/Society_Work_Department "Society Work Department"), which handles work related to civic groups, chambers of commerce and industry groups and mixed-ownership and non-public enterprises,[[162]](#cite_note-167) and the [Central Political and Legal Affairs Commission](/wiki/Central_Political_and_Legal_Affairs_Commission "Central Political and Legal Affairs Commission"), which oversees the country's legal enforcement authorities.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGuo2012b99,_237-168) The CC also has direct control over the [Central Policy Research Office](/wiki/Central_Policy_Research_Office "Central Policy Research Office"), which is responsible for researching issues of significant interest to the party leadership,[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFiner200343-169) the [Central Party School](/wiki/Central_Party_School "Central Party School"), which provides political training and ideological indoctrination in communist thought for high-ranking and rising cadres,[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan201249-170) the [Institute of Party History and Literature](/wiki/Institute_of_Party_History_and_Literature "Institute of Party History and Literature"), which sets priorities for scholarly research in state-run universities and the Central Party School and studies and translates the classical works of Marxism.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChambers200237-171)[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYu2010viii-172) The party's newspaper, the *People's Daily*, is under the direct control of the Central Committee[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELatham2007124-173) and is published with the objectives "to tell good stories about China and the (Party)" and to promote its party leader.[[169]](#cite_note-174) The theoretical magazines *[Qiushi](/wiki/Qiushi "Qiushi")* and *[Study Times](/wiki/Study_Times "Study Times")* are published by the Central Party School.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan201249-170) The [China Media Group](/wiki/China_Media_Group "China Media Group"), which oversees [China Central Television](/wiki/China_Central_Television "China Central Television") (CCTV), [China National Radio](/wiki/China_National_Radio "China National Radio") (CNR) and [China Radio International](/wiki/China_Radio_International "China Radio International") (CRI), is under the direct control of the Publicity Department.[[170]](#cite_note-175) The various offices of the Commissions and "Central Leading Groups", such as the [Hong Kong and Macao Affairs Office](/wiki/Hong_Kong_and_Macao_Affairs_Office "Hong Kong and Macao Affairs Office"), the [Taiwan Affairs Office](/wiki/Taiwan_Affairs_Office "Taiwan Affairs Office"), and the [Central Finance Office](/wiki/Office_of_the_Central_Financial_and_Economic_Affairs_Commission "Office of the Central Financial and Economic Affairs Commission"), also report to the central committee during a plenary session.[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeath2014141-176) Additionally, per the principle of "[the Party commands the gun](/wiki/The_Party_commands_the_gun "The Party commands the gun")", CCP has sole control over the People's Liberation Army (PLA) through its Central Military Commission.[[172]](#cite_note-177)

### Lower-level organizations

After seizing political power, the CCP extended the dual party-state command system to all government institutions, social organizations, and economic entities.[[173]](#cite_note-178) The State Council and the Supreme Court each has a [party group](/wiki/Party_group "Party group"), established since November 1949. Party committees permeate in every state administrative organ as well as the People's Consultation Conferences and mass organizations at all levels.[[174]](#cite_note-:10-179) According to scholar [Rush Doshi](/wiki/Rush_Doshi "Rush Doshi"), "[t]he Party sits above the state, runs parallel to the state, and is enmeshed in every level of the state."[[174]](#cite_note-:10-179) Modelled after the Soviet [Nomenklatura](/wiki/Nomenklatura "Nomenklatura") system, the party committee's organization department at each level has the power to recruit, train, monitor, appoint, and relocate these officials.[[175]](#cite_note-180) Party committees exist at the level of [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_China "Provinces of China"), cities, [counties](/wiki/County_(China) "County (China)"), and neighborhoods.[[176]](#cite_note-181) These committees play a key role in directing local policy by selecting local leaders and assigning critical tasks.[[177]](#cite_note-:1-182)[[178]](#cite_note-:3-183) The [CCP committee secretary](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party_Committee_Secretary "Chinese Communist Party Committee Secretary") at each level is more senior than that of the leader of the state, with the CCP standing committee being the main source of power.[[178]](#cite_note-:3-183) Party committee members in each level are selected by the leadership in the level above, with provincial leaders selected by the central Organizational Department, and not removable by the local party secretary.[[178]](#cite_note-:3-183) Neighborhood committees are generally composed of older volunteers.[[179]](#cite_note-:024-184): 118

CCP committees exist inside of companies, both private and state-owned.[[180]](#cite_note-185) A business that has more than three party members is legally required to establish a committee or branch.[[181]](#cite_note-:11-186)[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarquisQiao2022227-187) As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Chinese_Communist_Party&action=edit), more than half of China's private firms have such organizations.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarquisQiao2022225-188) These branches provide places for new member socialization and host morale boosting events for existing members.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarquisQiao202214-189) They also provide mechanisms that help private firms interface with government bodies and learn about policies which relate to their fields.[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarquisQiao2022225–226-190) On average, the profitability of private firms with a CCP branch is 12.6 per cent higher than the profitability of private firms.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarquisQiao2022230-191) Within [state-owned enterprises](/wiki/State-owned_enterprises_of_China "State-owned enterprises of China"), these branches are governing bodies that make important decisions and inculcate CCP ideology in employees.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarquisQiao202215-192) CCP committees or branches within companies also provide various benefits to employees.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarquisQiao2022228–229-193) These may include bonuses, interest-free loans, mentorship programs, and free medical and other services for those in need.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarquisQiao2022228–229-193) Enterprises that have party branches generally provide more expansive benefits for employees in the areas of retirement, medical care, unemployment, injury, and birth and fertility.[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarquisQiao2022229-194) Increasingly, the CCP is requiring private companies to revise their charters to include the role of the party.[[181]](#cite_note-:11-186)

### Funding

The funding of all CCP organizations mainly comes from state fiscal revenue. Data for the proportion of total CCP organizations' expenditures in total China fiscal revenue is unavailable.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Members

> "It is my will to join the Communist Party of China, uphold the Party's program, observe the provisions of the Party constitution, fulfill a Party member's duties, carry out the Party's decisions, strictly observe Party discipline, guard Party secrets, be loyal to the Party, work hard, fight for communism throughout my life, be ready at all times to sacrifice my all for the Party and the people, and never betray the Party."

— [Chinese Communist Party Admission Oath](/wiki/Chinese_Communist_Party_Admission_Oath "Chinese Communist Party Admission Oath")[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012183-195)

The CCP reached 100.27 million members at the end of 2024, a net increase of 1.09 million over the previous year.[[191]](#cite_note-2024PartyCensus-196)[[192]](#cite_note-:8-197) It is the second largest political party in the world.[[193]](#cite_note-198)

To join the CCP, an applicant must go through an approval process.[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarquisQiao202253–56-199) Adults can file applications for membership with their local party branch.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarquisQiao202253-200) A prescreening process, akin to a background check, follows.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarquisQiao202253-200) Next, established party members at the local branch vet applicants' behaviour and political attitudes and may make a formal inquiry to a party branch near the applicants' parents residence to vet family loyalty to communism and the party.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarquisQiao202253-200) In 2014, only 2 million applications were accepted out of some 22 million applicants.[[196]](#cite_note-:4-201) Admitted members then spend a year as a probationary member.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012183-195) Probationary members are typically accepted into the party.[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarquisQiao202255-202)

In contrast to the past, when emphasis was placed on the applicants' ideological criteria, the current CCP stresses technical and educational qualifications.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012183-195) To become a probationary member, the applicant must take an admission oath before the party flag.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012183-195) The relevant CCP organization is responsible for observing and educating probationary members.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012183-195) Probationary members have duties similar to those of full members, with the exception that they may not vote in party elections nor stand for election.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012183-195) Many join the CCP through the [Communist Youth League](/wiki/Communist_Youth_League_of_China "Communist Youth League of China").[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012183-195) Under Jiang Zemin, private entrepreneurs were allowed to become party members.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2012183-195)

#### Membership demographics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Chinese_Communist_Party_member_badge_diagram.png/250px-Chinese_Communist_Party_member_badge_diagram.png)](/wiki/File:Chinese_Communist_Party_member_badge_diagram.png)

Badge worn by party members

As of December 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Chinese_Communist_Party&action=edit), individuals who identify as farmers, herdsmen and fishermen make up 26.1 million members; members identifying as workers totalled 6.6 million.[[196]](#cite_note-:4-201)[[191]](#cite_note-2024PartyCensus-196) Another group, the "Managing, professional and technical staff in enterprises and public institutions", made up 16.4 million, 11.6 million identified as working in administrative staff and 7.6 million described themselves as party cadres.[[198]](#cite_note-CCPmembersoccupation-203) The CCP systematically recruits [white-collar workers](/wiki/White-collar_worker "White-collar worker") over other social groups.[[199]](#cite_note-204) By 2024, CCP membership had become more educated, younger, and less blue-collar than previously, with 57.6% of party members having a college degree or above.[[192]](#cite_note-:8-197) As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Chinese_Communist_Party&action=edit), around 30 to 35 per cent of Chinese entrepreneurs are or have been a party member.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarquisQiao202213-205) At the end of 2024, the CCP stated that it has approximately 7.73 million ethnic minority members or 7.7% of the party.[[191]](#cite_note-2024PartyCensus-196)

#### Status of women

As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Chinese_Communist_Party&action=edit), 30.99 million women are CCP members, representing 30.9% of the party.[[191]](#cite_note-2024PartyCensus-196) Women in China have low participation rates as political leaders. Women's disadvantage is most evident in their severe underrepresentation in the more powerful political positions.[[201]](#cite_note-Bauer-206) At the top level of decision making, no woman has ever been among the members of the Politburo Standing Committee, while the broader Politburo currently does not have any female members. Just 2 of 26 government ministers are women, and importantly, since 1997, China has fallen to 53rd place from 16th in the world in terms of female representation in the National People's Congress, according to the Inter-Parliamentary Union.[[202]](#cite_note-207) CCP leaders such as Zhao Ziyang have vigorously opposed the participation of women in the political process.[[203]](#cite_note-208) Within the party women face a [glass ceiling](/wiki/Glass_ceiling "Glass ceiling").[[204]](#cite_note-209)

#### Benefits and responsibilities of membership

A 2019 [Binghamton University](/wiki/Binghamton_University "Binghamton University") study found that CCP members gain a 20% wage premium in the market over non-members.[[205]](#cite_note-:03-210) A subsequent academic study found that the economic benefit of CCP membership is strongest on those in lower wealth brackets.[[205]](#cite_note-:03-210) CCP households also tend to accumulate wealth faster than non-CCP households.[[206]](#cite_note-211) Certain CCP cadres have access to a [special supply system](/wiki/Special_supply_system_of_China "Special supply system of China") for foodstuffs called *tegong*.[[207]](#cite_note-212) CCP leadership cadres have access to a dedicated [healthcare](/wiki/Healthcare_in_China "Healthcare in China") system managed by the CCP General Office.[[208]](#cite_note-213)

CCP membership additionally comes with several responsibilities. CCP members are required to give between 0.5% and 2% of their monthly salary as membership fee, which goes to the party funds.[[209]](#cite_note-214) Members must pay dues regardless of location. They additionally need to regularly attend party meetings and basic organizational cell activities.[[192]](#cite_note-:8-197) In 2019, the CCP Central Committee issued a rule requiring members abroad to contact CCP cells at home at least once every six months.[[210]](#cite_note-215) Failure to pay dues for six consecutive months is grounds for expulsion, according to party regulations.[[211]](#cite_note-216)

#### Personality traits

A 2021 analysis by [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") academic Rory Truex of survey results showed that CCP members on average had very high levels of [extraversion](/wiki/Extraversion_and_introversion "Extraversion and introversion"), [agreeableness](/wiki/Agreeableness "Agreeableness"), and [conscientiousness](/wiki/Conscientiousness "Conscientiousness"), and exhibited more traits associated with personal and professional success such as confidence, organization and work ethic, interpersonal skill, and creativity and dynamism. The survey results suggest that this contributes to the CCP's continuing dominance by relegating people discontented with CCP to the margins of society.[[212]](#cite_note-217)

#### Religion

Further information: [State atheism](/wiki/State_atheism "State atheism") and [Antireligious campaigns in China](/wiki/Antireligious_campaigns_in_China "Antireligious campaigns in China")

The CCP requires its members to be atheists. Party regulations state that religious members are to be given a chance to renounce their beliefs and be expelled if they do not.[[213]](#cite_note-218) Cadres that actively practice religion or frequent fortune-tellers have faced investigation and expulsion from the CCP.[[214]](#cite_note-219)[[215]](#cite_note-220)

### Communist Youth League

Main article: [Communist Youth League of China](/wiki/Communist_Youth_League_of_China "Communist Youth League of China")

The Communist Youth League (CYL) is the CCP's [youth wing](/wiki/Youth_wing "Youth wing"), and the largest mass organization for youth in China.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2007582-221) To join, an applicant has to be between the ages of 14 and 28.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2007582-221) It controls and supervises [Young Pioneers](/wiki/Young_Pioneers_of_China "Young Pioneers of China"), a youth organization for children below the age of 14.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2007582-221) The organizational structure of CYL is an exact copy of the CCP's; the highest body is the National Congress, followed by the Central Committee, Politburo, and the Politburo Standing Committee.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESullivan2007583-222) However, the Central Committee (and all central organs) of the CYL work under the guidance of the CCP central leadership.[[218]](#cite_note-CPCactualconstitution-223) By the end of 2024, the CYLC had 75 million members and 4.4 million organizations throughout China.[[219]](#cite_note-:23-224)

## Symbols

Main article: [Emblem and flag of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/Emblem_and_flag_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Emblem and flag of the Chinese Communist Party")

At the beginning of its history, the CCP did not have a single official standard for the flag, but instead allowed individual party committees to copy the flag of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union.[[220]](#cite_note-CPCemblemflag-225) The Central Politburo decreed the establishment of a sole official flag on 28 April 1942: "The flag of the CPC has the length-to-width proportion of 3:2 with a [hammer and sickle](/wiki/Hammer_and_sickle "Hammer and sickle") in the upper-left corner, and with no five-pointed star. The Political Bureau authorizes the General Office to custom-make a number of standard flags and distribute them to all major organs".[[220]](#cite_note-CPCemblemflag-225) According to the *People's Daily*, "The red color symbolizes revolution; the hammer-and-sickle are tools of workers and peasants, meaning that the Communist Party of China represents the interests of the masses and the people; the yellow color signifies brightness."[[220]](#cite_note-CPCemblemflag-225) The CCP does not have an official song, though [The Internationale](/wiki/The_Internationale "The Internationale") is used in certain events.[[221]](#cite_note-226)

## Party-to-party relations

The [International Department of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/International_Department_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "International Department of the Chinese Communist Party") is responsible for dialogue with global political parties.[[222]](#cite_note-227)

### Communist parties

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Xi_Jinping_and_Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Ph%C3%BA_Tr%E1%BB%8Dng_2023.jpg/250px-Xi_Jinping_and_Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Ph%C3%BA_Tr%E1%BB%8Dng_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xi_Jinping_and_Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Ph%C3%BA_Tr%E1%BB%8Dng_2023.jpg)

Xi Jinping and Communist Party of Vietnam General Secretary [Nguyễn Phú Trọng](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Ph%C3%BA_Tr%E1%BB%8Dng "Nguyễn Phú Trọng") in December 2023

The CCP continues to have relations with non-ruling communist and workers' parties and attends international communist conferences, most notably the [International Meeting of Communist and Workers' Parties](/wiki/International_Meeting_of_Communist_and_Workers%27_Parties "International Meeting of Communist and Workers' Parties").[[223]](#cite_note-228)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] While the CCP retains contact with major parties such as the [Communist Party of Portugal](/wiki/Portuguese_Communist_Party "Portuguese Communist Party"),[[224]](#cite_note-PCP-229) the [Communist Party of France](/wiki/French_Communist_Party "French Communist Party"),[[225]](#cite_note-FCP-230) the [Communist Party of the Russian Federation](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Russian_Federation "Communist Party of the Russian Federation"),[[226]](#cite_note-231) the [Communist Party of Bohemia and Moravia](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Bohemia_and_Moravia "Communist Party of Bohemia and Moravia"),[[227]](#cite_note-232) the [Communist Party of Brazil](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Brazil "Communist Party of Brazil"),[[228]](#cite_note-233) the [Communist Party of Greece](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Greece "Communist Party of Greece"),[[229]](#cite_note-234) the [Communist Party of Nepal (UML)](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Nepal_(Unified_Marxist%E2%88%92Leninist) "Communist Party of Nepal (Unified Marxist−Leninist)")[[230]](#cite_note-235) and the [Communist Party of Spain](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Spain "Communist Party of Spain"),[[231]](#cite_note-236) the party also retains relations with minor communist and workers' parties, such as the [Communist Party of Australia](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Australia "Communist Party of Australia"),[[232]](#cite_note-237) the [Communist Party of Bangladesh](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Bangladesh "Communist Party of Bangladesh"),[[233]](#cite_note-238) the [Workers Party of Bangladesh](/wiki/Workers_Party_of_Bangladesh "Workers Party of Bangladesh"), the [Communist Party of Sri Lanka](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Sri_Lanka "Communist Party of Sri Lanka"), the [Workers' Party of Belgium](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_of_Belgium "Workers' Party of Belgium"), the [Hungarian Workers' Party](/wiki/Hungarian_Workers%27_Party "Hungarian Workers' Party"), the [Dominican Workers' Party](/wiki/Dominican_Workers%27_Party "Dominican Workers' Party"), the [Nepal Workers Peasants Party](/wiki/Nepal_Workers_Peasants_Party "Nepal Workers Peasants Party"), and the [Party for the Transformation of Honduras](/wiki/Party_for_the_Transformation_of_Honduras "Party for the Transformation of Honduras"), for instance.[[234]](#cite_note-239) It has poor relations with the [Japanese Communist Party](/wiki/Japanese_Communist_Party "Japanese Communist Party").[[235]](#cite_note-240) In recent years, noting the self-reform of the European social democratic movement in the 1980s and 1990s, the CCP "has noted the increased marginalization of West European communist parties."[[236]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh2008100-241)

#### Ruling parties of socialist states

The CCP has retained close relations with the ruling parties of [socialist states](/wiki/Socialist_state "Socialist state") still espousing [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism"): [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), [Laos](/wiki/Laos "Laos"), [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea"), and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam").[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200881-242) It spends a fair amount of time analysing the situation in the remaining socialist states, trying to reach conclusions as to why these states survived when so many did not, following the [collapse of the Eastern European socialist states](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1989 "Revolutions of 1989") in 1989 and the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200882-243) In general, the analyses of the remaining socialist states and their chances of survival have been positive, and the CCP believes that the socialist movement will be revitalized sometime in the future.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200882-243)

The ruling party which the CCP is most interested in is the [Communist Party of Vietnam](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Vietnam "Communist Party of Vietnam") (CPV).[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200884-244) In general the CPV is considered a model example of socialist development in the post-Soviet era.[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200884-244) Chinese analysts on Vietnam believe that the introduction of the [Đổi Mới](/wiki/%C4%90%E1%BB%95i_M%E1%BB%9Bi "Đổi Mới") reform policy at the [6th CPV National Congress](/wiki/6th_National_Congress_of_the_Communist_Party_of_Vietnam "6th National Congress of the Communist Party of Vietnam") is the key reason for Vietnam's current success.[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200884-244)

While the CCP is probably the organization with [most access to North Korea](/wiki/China%E2%80%93North_Korea_relations "China–North Korea relations"), writing about North Korea is tightly circumscribed.[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200882-243) The few reports accessible to the general public are those about [North Korean economic reforms](/wiki/North_Korean_economic_reform "North Korean economic reform").[[238]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200882-243) While Chinese analysts of North Korea tend to speak positively of North Korea in public, in official discussions c. 2008 they show much disdain for [North Korea's economic system](/wiki/Economy_of_North_Korea "Economy of North Korea"), the [cult of personality](/wiki/North_Korean_cult_of_personality "North Korean cult of personality") which pervades society, the [Kim family](/wiki/Kim_dynasty_(North_Korea) "Kim dynasty (North Korea)"), the idea of hereditary succession in a socialist state, the security state, the use of scarce resources on the [Korean People's Army](/wiki/Korean_People%27s_Army "Korean People's Army") and the general impoverishment of the North Korean people.[[240]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200882–83-245) Circa 2008, there are those analysts who compare the current situation of North Korea with that of China during the Cultural Revolution.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200883-246)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] Over the years, the CCP has tried to persuade the [Workers' Party of Korea](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_of_Korea "Workers' Party of Korea") (or WPK, North Korea's ruling party) to introduce economic reforms by showing them key economic infrastructure in China.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200883-246) For instance, in 2006 the CCP invited then-[WPK general secretary](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Workers%27_Party_of_Korea "General Secretary of the Workers' Party of Korea") [Kim Jong Il](/wiki/Kim_Jong_Il "Kim Jong Il") to [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong") to showcase the success economic reforms had brought China.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200883-246) In general, the CCP considers the WPK and North Korea to be negative examples of a ruling communist party and socialist state.[[241]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200883-246)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

There is a considerable degree of interest in Cuba within the CCP.[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200884-244) [Fidel Castro](/wiki/Fidel_Castro "Fidel Castro"), the former [First Secretary](/wiki/First_Secretary_of_the_Communist_Party_of_Cuba "First Secretary of the Communist Party of Cuba") of the [Communist Party of Cuba](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Cuba "Communist Party of Cuba") (PCC), is greatly admired, and books have been written focusing on the successes of the [Cuban Revolution](/wiki/Cuban_Revolution "Cuban Revolution").[[239]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200884-244) [Communication between the CCP and the PCC](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Cuba_relations "China–Cuba relations") has increased since the 1990s.[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200885-247) At the 4th Plenary Session of the 16th Central Committee, which discussed the possibility of the CCP learning from other ruling parties, praise was heaped on the PCC.[[242]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200885-247) When [Wu Guanzheng](/wiki/Wu_Guanzheng "Wu Guanzheng"), a Central Politburo member, met with Fidel Castro in 2007, he gave him a personal letter written by Hu Jintao: "Facts have shown that China and Cuba are trustworthy good friends, good comrades, and good brothers who treat each other with sincerity. The two countries' friendship has withstood the test of a changeable international situation, and the friendship has been further strengthened and consolidated."[[243]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200885–86-248)

### Non-communist parties

Since the decline and fall of communism in Eastern Europe, the CCP has begun establishing party-to-party relations with non-communist parties.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmithWest2012127-166) These relations are sought so that the CCP can learn from them.[[244]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200886–92-249) For instance, the CCP has been eager to understand how the [People's Action Party](/wiki/People%27s_Action_Party "People's Action Party") of Singapore (PAP) maintains its total domination over [Singaporean politics](/wiki/Politics_of_Singapore "Politics of Singapore") through its "low-key presence, but total control."[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200893-250) According to the CCP's own analysis of Singapore, the PAP's dominance can be explained by its "well-developed social network, which controls constituencies effectively by extending its tentacles deeply into society through branches of government and party-controlled groups."[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200893-250) While the CCP accepts that Singapore is a [liberal democracy](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy"), they view it as a [guided democracy](/wiki/Guided_democracy "Guided democracy") led by the PAP.[[245]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200893-250) Other differences are, according to the CCP, "that it is not a political party based on the working class—instead it is a political party of the elite. ... It is also a political party of the [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system"), not a [revolutionary party](/wiki/Vanguard_party "Vanguard party")."[[246]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200894-251) Other parties which the CCP studies and maintains strong party-to-party relations with are the [United Malays National Organization](/wiki/United_Malays_National_Organization "United Malays National Organization"), which has ruled [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") (1957–2018, 2020–2022), and the [Liberal Democratic Party](/wiki/Liberal_Democratic_Party_(Japan) "Liberal Democratic Party (Japan)") in Japan, which dominated [Japanese politics](/wiki/Japanese_politics "Japanese politics") since 1955.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200895–96-252)

Since Jiang Zemin's time, the CCP has made friendly overtures to its erstwhile foe, the Kuomintang. The CCP emphasizes [strong party-to-party relations with the KMT](/wiki/Relations_between_the_Kuomintang_and_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "Relations between the Kuomintang and the Chinese Communist Party") so as to strengthen the probability of the reunification of Taiwan with mainland China.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200896-253) However, several studies have been written on the KMT's loss of power in 2000 after having ruled Taiwan since 1949 (the KMT officially ruled mainland China from 1928 to 1949).[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200896-253) In general, one-party states or dominant-party states are of special interest to the party and party-to-party relations are formed so that the CCP can study them.[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200896-253) The longevity of the [Syrian Regional Branch](/wiki/Arab_Socialist_Ba%27ath_Party_%E2%80%93_Syria_Region "Arab Socialist Ba'ath Party – Syria Region") of the [Arab Socialist Ba'ath Party](/wiki/Ba%27ath_Party_(Syrian-dominated_faction) "Ba'ath Party (Syrian-dominated faction)") is attributed to the personalization of power in the [al-Assad family](/wiki/Al-Assad_family "Al-Assad family"), the strong [presidential system](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system"), the inheritance of power, which passed from [Hafez al-Assad](/wiki/Hafez_al-Assad "Hafez al-Assad") to his son [Bashar al-Assad](/wiki/Bashar_al-Assad "Bashar al-Assad"), and the role given to the [Syrian military](/wiki/Syrian_military "Syrian military") in politics.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200897-254)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Cena_de_Estado_que_en_honor_del_Excmo._Sr._Xi_Jinping%2C_Presidente_de_la_Rep%C3%BAblica_Popular_China%2C_y_de_su_esposa%2C_Sra._Peng_Liyuan_%288959188037%29.jpg/250px-Cena_de_Estado_que_en_honor_del_Excmo._Sr._Xi_Jinping%2C_Presidente_de_la_Rep%C3%BAblica_Popular_China%2C_y_de_su_esposa%2C_Sra._Peng_Liyuan_%288959188037%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cena_de_Estado_que_en_honor_del_Excmo._Sr._Xi_Jinping,_Presidente_de_la_Rep%C3%BAblica_Popular_China,_y_de_su_esposa,_Sra._Peng_Liyuan_(8959188037).jpg)

Xi Jinping (second from left) with [Enrique Peña Nieto](/wiki/Enrique_Pe%C3%B1a_Nieto "Enrique Peña Nieto") (second from right), the former [President of Mexico](/wiki/President_of_Mexico "President of Mexico") and a leading member of the Institutional Revolutionary Party

Circa 2008, the CCP has been especially interested in Latin America,[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200897-254) as shown by the increasing number of delegates sent to and received from these countries.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200897-254) Of special fascination for the CCP is the 71-year-long rule of the [Institutional Revolutionary Party](/wiki/Institutional_Revolutionary_Party "Institutional Revolutionary Party") (PRI) in Mexico.[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200897-254) While the CCP attributed the PRI's long reign in power to the strong presidential system, tapping into the [machismo](/wiki/Machismo "Machismo") culture of the country, its nationalist posture, its close identification with the rural populace and the implementation of [nationalization](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization") alongside the [marketization](/wiki/Marketization "Marketization") of the [economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Mexico "Economy of Mexico"),[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200897-254) the CCP concluded that the PRI failed because of the lack of inner-party democracy, its pursuit of [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy"), its rigid party structures that could not be reformed, its [political corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Mexico "Corruption in Mexico"), the pressure of globalization, and American interference in [Mexican politics](/wiki/Mexican_politics "Mexican politics").[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200897-254) While the CCP was slow to recognize the [pink tide](/wiki/Pink_tide "Pink tide") in Latin America, it has strengthened party-to-party relations with several socialist and [anti-American](/wiki/Anti-American "Anti-American") political parties over the years.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200898-255) The CCP has occasionally expressed some irritation over [Hugo Chávez](/wiki/Hugo_Ch%C3%A1vez "Hugo Chávez")'s [anti-capitalist](/wiki/Anti-capitalist "Anti-capitalist") and anti-American rhetoric.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200898-255) Despite this, the CCP reached an agreement in 2013 with the [United Socialist Party of Venezuela](/wiki/United_Socialist_Party_of_Venezuela "United Socialist Party of Venezuela") (PSUV), which was founded by Chávez, for the CCP to educate PSUV cadres in political and social fields.[[251]](#cite_note-256) By 2008, the CCP claimed to have established relations with 99 political parties in 29 Latin American countries.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200898-255)

Social democratic movements in Europe have been of great interest to the CCP since the early 1980s.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200898-255) In 1984, the [Social Democratic Party of Germany](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") (SPD) initiated party dialogues with the CCP, becoming the first international party relationship CCP established outside of other communist parties.[[252]](#cite_note-257) With the exception of a short period in which the CCP forged party-to-party relations with far-right parties during the 1970s in an effort to halt "[Soviet expansionism](/wiki/Soviet_empire "Soviet empire")", the CCP's relations with European social democratic parties were its first serious efforts to establish cordial party-to-party relations with non-communist parties.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200898-255) The CCP credits the European social democrats with creating a "capitalism with a human face".[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200898-255) Before the 1980s, the CCP had a highly negative and dismissive view of social democracy, a view dating back to the [Second International](/wiki/Second_International "Second International") and the Marxist–Leninist view on the social democratic movement.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200898-255) By the 1980s, that view had changed and the CCP concluded that it could actually learn something from the social democratic movement.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200898-255) CCP delegates were sent all over Europe to observe.[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200899-258) By the 1980s, most European social democratic parties were facing electoral decline and in a period of self-reform.[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200899-258) The CCP followed this with great interest, laying most weight on reform efforts within the [British Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") and the SPD.[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200899-258) The CCP concluded that both parties were re-elected because they modernized, replacing traditional [state socialist](/wiki/State_socialist "State socialist") tenets with new ones supporting [privatization](/wiki/Privatization "Privatization"), shedding the belief in [big government](/wiki/Big_government "Big government"), conceiving a new view of the [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state"), changing their negative views of the market and moving from their traditional support base of trade unions to entrepreneurs, the young and students.[[254]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShambaugh200899–100-259)
